title: Zeroes of Characters (cf. J.-P. Serre, same title, arXiv 2312.17551)
speaker: Pierre Deligne
abstract: A promenade in Serre's paper, plus some related results of mine.

proof_stages.label: The zero-of-character theorem and variants
proof_stages.summary: The talk states that every non-one-dimensional irreducible representation of a complex connected reductive group has a character vanishing at some group element, and records compact-Lie, arbitrary linear-group, and prime-power-order variants.

proof_stages.label: Finite-group Burnside analogue
proof_stages.summary: As motivation and as an ingredient for later extensions, the finite-group theorem is proved first in the rational-valued case by character orthogonality and then in general by grouping generators of a cyclic subgroup into Galois conjugacy classes and applying arithmetic-geometric mean reasoning to norms.

proof_stages.label: Root datum and rho for a connected reductive group
proof_stages.summary: The proof reduces character questions to a maximal torus and sets up characters, cocharacters, roots, coroots, Weyl symmetry, positive roots, and the half-sum rho, emphasizing the duality between roots and coroots.

proof_stages.label: Weyl character formula and the special product case
proof_stages.summary: The Weyl character formula is recalled in lambda-plus-rho form, its denominator identity is isolated, and the special case lambda+rho being a multiple of rho yields an explicit product formula whose zero locus is immediately visible.

proof_stages.label: Dual one-parameter subgroup for arbitrary highest weight
proof_stages.summary: Using the symmetry of the root datum, the special product-formula argument is dualized: restricting any irreducible character to the cocharacter rho-dual gives a quotient of products of cyclotomic-type factors, forcing a root-of-unity zero.

proof_stages.label: Prime-power-order refinement
proof_stages.summary: The product formula is sharpened by comparing numerator and denominator exponents, reducing to prime-power cyclotomic factors, and proving a partition/transposed-partition lemma that guarantees a zero of prime-power order.

proof_stages.label: Mechanisms and open structure of zero loci
proof_stages.summary: After the proof, the speaker discusses how torsion zeros on tori should arise from finite unions of translated subtori, reviews known mechanisms for producing zeros, and notes that computations, especially in G2, reveal additional unexplained zeros.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to disconnected or general linear groups
proof_stages.summary: In response to a question, the speaker explains the extension beyond the connected component by restricting to G^0, decomposing into isotypic components permuted transitively by a finite quotient, using fixed-point-free elements and Burnside-type finite-group input, and treating the remaining isotypic cases separately.

examples.name: Classical reductive groups
examples.note: GL_n, SO_n, Sp_n and exceptional groups are named to make concrete the class of complex connected reductive groups covered by the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: Classical groups such as GL_n, SO_n and Sp_n are only listed as motivating examples of reductive groups, with no separate work done on them as a class.

examples.name: GL_n diagonal torus and root datum
examples.note: The speaker repeatedly translates maximal tori, character lattices, roots, coroots, positive roots and rho into diagonal matrices in GL_n to make the general root-datum definitions visible.
examples.engagement_reason: For GL_n he explicitly takes the diagonal torus, identifies X(T) and cocharacters as Z^n, computes the roots from matrix entries, describes coroots via embedded SL_2's, and identifies the Weyl group.

examples.name: Vandermonde form of the GL_n Weyl formula
examples.note: For GL_n the Weyl group is the symmetric group and the alternating Weyl sums become determinants, illustrating the Weyl denominator identity by the Vandermonde product formula.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes the Weyl character formula to GL_n, rewrites the Weyl sum as essentially a Vandermonde determinant, derives the product form, and reads off zero loci from the factors.

passes.reason: The Vandermonde form is the fully S_n-alternating regular product/determinant form used constructively in the Weyl formula.
the Vandermonde determinant is the fully S_n-alternating regular factorization used to compute the Weyl formula
The Vandermonde/Weyl denominator is a highly regular S_n-alternating product over all pairs, used constructively in the formula.
The Vandermonde form is the highly regular S_n-alternating product/determinant form of the GL_n Weyl formula.
The Vandermonde/Weyl-denominator form is the highly regular alternating S_n-symmetric product formula, used constructively in the character formula.
The Vandermonde form is the regular S_n-alternating product over all pairwise coordinate differences.
The Vandermonde Weyl numerator is the regular alternating product/determinant under the symmetric-group action.
the Vandermonde determinant is the S_n-alternating product form of the GL_n Weyl formula
The Vandermonde form is used constructively, with full S_n alternation and product-over-pairs regularity.
The Vandermonde form is the highly regular alternating S_n-expression of the GL_n Weyl formula, used computationally rather than adversarially.
the Vandermonde alternant is singled out by its full S_n anti-symmetry and regular product factorization
The Vandermonde determinant is the fully S_n-alternating regular form of the GL_n Weyl denominator/numerator.

examples.name: SL_3 rho-dual one-parameter subgroup
examples.note: In type A_2 the distinguished cocharacter is displayed as the concrete subgroup like diag(u,1,u^{-1}), illustrating the general restriction to rho-dual.
examples.engagement_reason: He identifies the rho-dual one-parameter subgroup in the SL_3 case and then runs the character-restriction/factorization argument on this rho-dual subgroup.

passes.reason: The SL_3 rho-dual subgroup is the canonical balanced one-parameter subgroup used to factor character restrictions.

examples.name: G2 computations with unexplained zeros
examples.note: The speaker says that computing examples in G2 kept revealing additional zeros, motivating the search for further mechanisms and showing that the listed explanations are incomplete.
examples.engagement_reason: G2 is cited as the subject of experiments where extra unexplained zeros appear, but no concrete G2 computation is shown in the talk.

examples.name: Two-root-length systems G2, B2/C2 and F4
examples.note: Root systems with two lengths illustrate another source of product formulas, since rescaling one length gives a second root system and hence another useful one-parameter subgroup.
examples.engagement_reason: For two-root-length systems he explains the construction of rescaling roots, gives the sqrt(3) and sqrt(2) cases for G2 and B2/C2/F4, and derives the resulting extra one-parameter subgroup with product-form restrictions.

passes.reason: The list includes the exceptional non-simply-laced types G2 and F4 alongside B2/C2, used as a positive two-root-length mechanism.
